---
title: "Pytest Parameterisation"
date: "2024-09-24"
categories: [python, testing, pytest, matplotlib, pytest-mpl]
image: https://live.staticflickr.com/65535/53258274023_f628d3291a_k.jpg
from: markdown+emoji
toc: true
toc-depth: 4
toc-location: right
execute:
  code_fold: true
  code_link: true
  code_tools: true
  fig-cap-location: top
  tbl-cap-location: top
  warning: false
---

[Pytest][pytest] is an excellent framework for writing tests in [Python][python]. Sometimes your code will generate
images using [Matplotlib][mpl] or related libraries based around it such as [Seaborn][seaborn] or [plotnine][plotnine]
and ideally your [pytest][pytest] suite should check that your functions generate the images you expect. There is an
excellent [pytest plugin][pytestplugin] to help with this [pytest-mpl][pytest-mpl] and this post covers how to use it
and a _very_ useful feature to help you investigate differences in images when your tests of images fail.


## Installation

Setup a new virtual environment, here I use [virtualenvwrapper][virtualenvwrapper] to create a temporary virtual
environment (which will be deleted on deactivation) and install the [`pytest-mpl`] package (it pulls in `matplotlib`,
`pytest` and a bunch of other dependencies).

```
mktmpenv
pip install pytest-mpl
```

## Plotting Function

We need a simple example to write tests for. As with previous posts I've made the code available in the
[pytest-examples][pytest-examples] repository where I've written a function under `pytestexample/mpl_example.py` to plot
a scatter plot of two random variables.

``` {.python}
#| label: python-test
#| code-fold: false
#| code-link: true
#| code-tools: true
#| eval: false
#| echo: true"""Example code for pytest-mpl exposition."""

import numpy as np
import numpy.typing as npt
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


def scatter(
    n_obs: int,
    figsize: tuple[int, int],
    title: str | None = None,
    cmap: str = "green",
    seed: int = 3513387,
) -> tuple:
    """Generate a scatter plot of two random variables.

    This example is taken from the `Scatter Histogram Example
    <https://matplotlib.org/stable/gallery/lines_bars_and_markers/scatter_hist.html>`_.

    Parameters
    ----------
    n_obs : int
        Number of random observations to generate.
    figsize : tuple[int, int]
        Shape to plot.
    cmap : str
        Colour to use for plotting.
    seed : int
        Seed for pseudo-random number generation.

    Returns
    -------
    tuple(fig, ax)

    """
    # Generate two random sets of numbers
    x = np.random.randn(n_obs)
    y = np.random.randn(n_obs)
    # Create the figure
    fig = plt.figure(figsize=figsize)
    ax = fig.add_subplot()
    ax.scatter(x, y)

    return (fig, ax)

```

We now need a test to check that for a given set of inputs the same output is always returned. Following the naming
conventions used by [pytest][pytest] we create this at `tests/test_example.py`. Whilst Matplotlib provides its own
[`image_comparison`][mpl-image-comparison] decorator from Matplotlib itself which requires at a bare minimum the
`baseline_image` I'm going to skim over its usage and instead discuss use of the [`pytest-mpl`][pytest-mpl] extensionas it offers a few extra features which are really neat. Instead of using the `@image_comparsion()` decorator we can add
the `pytest-mpl` package to the optional `test` dependencies in `pyproject.toml`

```toml
[project.optional-dependencies]
tests = [
  "py",
  "pytest",
  "pytest-cov",
  "pytest-mpl",
  "pytest-tmp-files",
]
```

This means we can use the `@pytest.mark.mpl_image_compare()` decorator to mark a test as having Matplotlib output
against which tests are compared. We need to set the `baseline_dir` which is where the images against which tests will
be compared are stored. We then call our function, in this case `mpl_example.scatter()` with different sets of
parameters (courtesy of [pytest parameterisation][pytest-param]).

The test itself must `return fig` of the desired plot so that it can be compared to the reference image each time the
test is run.

``` {.python}
#| label: python-test
#| code-fold: false
#| code-link: true
#| code-tools: true
#| eval: false
#| echo: trueimport pytest
"""Tests of the mpl_example module."""

import pytest

from pytest_examples import mpl_example


@pytest.mark.mpl_image_compare(baseline_dir="resources/img")
@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    ("n_obs", "figsize", "title", "cmap", "seed"),
    [
        pytest.param(300, (6, 6), None, "green", 3513387, id="300 points; 6x6; no title; green"),
        pytest.param(3000, (6, 6), "Lots of points!", "green", 3513387, id="3000 points; 6x6; Lots of points; blue"),
    ],
)
def test_scatter_hist(n_obs: int, figsize: tuple[int, int], title: str, cmap: str, seed: int) -> None:
    """Test of the scatter_hist() function."""
    fig, ax = mpl_example.scatter(n_obs, figsize, title, cmap, seed)
    return fig

```

Once you have decorated your test you need to generate the images against which subsequent tests are to be compared
to. This is done using the `--mpl-generate-path=` flag and providing an appropriate argument. The path is relative to
the `tests/` directory and should match what you supply to the `baseline_dir` argument of the decorator. You will have
to create the directory first though and I typically use `tests/resources/img`. After creating the reference images you
will want to add them to Git so they are available whenever the rests run.

```bash
mkdir -p tests/resources/img
pytest --mpl-generate-path=tests/resources/img
git add tests/resources/img/*
git commit -m "tests: Adding reference images for Matplotlib based tests."

```

Each time you run your test suite the output of calling the test is compared to the reference images that reside under
`tests/resources/img` (or whatever directory you specified).

## `--mpl-generate-summary`

Sometimes your tests might fail, but when the tests that fail are images not matching there is a _really_ neat option
to `pytest-mpl` that helps determine where the problem may lie. This is the
`--mpl-generate-summary={html,json,basic-html}` option. If you use the `html` option to this flag/argument then a
summary in HTML of the tests that use the `@pytest.mark.mpl_image_compare()` decorator is generated and can be viewed in
a browser to make it relatively easy to view what has changed.

To demonstrate this we change the value of one of `seed` for one of the parameters from `3513387` to something else. By
default the report is created under `/tmp/` but the `--mpl-results-path` you can specify a location relative to where
the tests are being run from. For example you could create the `mpl-test` directory and have results saved there.

```sh
mkdir mpl-test
echo "mpl-test/" >> .gitignore
git add .gitignore && git commit -m "chore: ignore mpl-test directory"
pytest --mpl-generate-summary=html --mpl-results-path=mpl-test tests/test_mpl_example.py
```



[mpl]: https://matplotlib.org/
[mpl-image-comparison]: https://matplotlib.org/devdocs/devel/testing.html#writing-an-image-comparison-test
[pytest]: https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/
[pytest-mpl]: https://pytest-mpl.readthedocs.io/en/stable/
[pytest-param]: https://blog.nshephard.dev/posts/pytest-param/
[python]: https://python.org
